description: The DDPM (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models) paper title/cover page, showing the original paper by Ho et al. that introduced the DDPM framework
teaching_value: Anchors the tutorial to its primary source, helping students locate the foundational paper and understand the academic context of the implementation
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on diffusion models to establish the academic source and motivate why this specific paper is being annotated
suggested_caption: DDPM paper by Ho et al. (2020), the basis for this annotated implementation.

description: A diagram illustrating the forward (noising) and reverse (denoising) diffusion processes, showing a sequence of images transitioning from a clean image x0 through progressively noisier states x1...xT and back, with arrows labeled for the forward process q and reverse process p
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the two-process structure of diffusion models — the forward Markov chain that adds Gaussian noise and the learned reverse process that removes it — making the abstract math concrete for students
when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the two-process framework, before presenting any equations, so students have an intuitive visual anchor for q(x_t|x_{t-1}) and p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t)
suggested_caption: Forward (noising) and reverse (denoising) processes in a diffusion model. (Ho et al., 2020)

description: A pseudocode block showing the training algorithm for a diffusion model: sample a real image x0, sample a random time step t, sample Gaussian noise ε, compute the noisy image x_t, and minimize MSE between true noise ε and predicted noise ε_θ(x_t, t)
teaching_value: Translates the mathematical objective into a concrete, step-by-step training loop, helping students understand exactly what the neural network is trained to predict and how the loss is computed in practice
when_to_show: Show after deriving the simplified MSE objective, to bridge theory and implementation; use it to walk through each line of the training loop before showing actual code
suggested_caption: Training algorithm: predict noise ε at random time step t, minimize MSE. (Ho et al., 2020)

description: A diagram of the U-Net architecture showing the encoder (downsampling) path on the left, bottleneck in the middle, and decoder (upsampling) path on the right, with skip connections between corresponding encoder and decoder layers
teaching_value: Illustrates the specific neural network architecture used as the denoising backbone, showing how skip connections preserve spatial information and how the bottleneck forces compact representations — critical for understanding why U-Net is chosen for this task
when_to_show: Show when introducing the neural network component of the diffusion model, after explaining the autoencoder analogy, to ground the architectural choice in a visual before discussing attention and time embeddings
suggested_caption: U-Net architecture with skip connections used as the diffusion model backbone. (Ronneberger et al., 2015)

description: A grid of cat images from ImageNet used as the real data distribution x0, showing the clean, uncorrupted input images before any noise is applied in the forward diffusion process
teaching_value: Provides a concrete visual reference for x0 (the real data distribution), helping students understand what the model is ultimately trying to reconstruct and what 'clean data' looks like before the forward process begins
when_to_show: Show when introducing the forward diffusion process and image preprocessing steps, to give students a concrete sense of the starting point before demonstrating how noise is progressively added
suggested_caption: Sample cat images (x₀) from the data distribution before any noise is added.

description: A single cat image shown after applying the forward diffusion process at a specific time step t, demonstrating the noisy image x_t at one particular noise level as a verification step
teaching_value: Verifies that the forward process implementation is correct by showing a single noisy sample, helping students check their understanding of how q(x_t|x_0) works at a chosen time step
when_to_show: Show immediately after presenting the forward diffusion function implementation, as a sanity-check example before visualizing multiple time steps
suggested_caption: Cat image after applying forward diffusion at a single time step t.

description: A sequence of cat images showing the same image at progressively increasing noise levels across multiple time steps (e.g., t=0, t=50, t=100, ..., t=T), illustrating how the forward diffusion process gradually destroys image structure
teaching_value: Powerfully demonstrates the forward Markov chain in action — students can see how image content is progressively replaced by Gaussian noise, building intuition for why the reverse process is non-trivial to learn
when_to_show: Show after the single time-step verification, when explaining the noise schedule and cumulative variance ᾱ_t, to illustrate how different β schedules affect the rate of information destruction
suggested_caption: Forward diffusion: cat image progressively corrupted by Gaussian noise over T time steps.

description: Another visualization of multiple noisy cat images at different time steps, likely showing a different noise schedule or a different set of time step samples compared to the previous figure, used to illustrate the forward process before defining the loss function
teaching_value: Reinforces the visual intuition for the forward process and directly precedes the loss function definition, helping students connect the noisy samples x_t to what the neural network must learn to invert
when_to_show: Show just before introducing the loss function, to motivate why predicting the noise ε is the training objective — students can see what the network must 'undo'
suggested_caption: Noisy images at various time steps, motivating the noise-prediction training objective.

description: A pseudocode block showing the sampling (inference) algorithm for a diffusion model: start from pure Gaussian noise x_T, then iteratively apply the learned reverse process p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t) for t=T down to t=1, adding noise at each step except the last
teaching_value: Shows students the exact inference procedure — how to generate new images by running the reverse Markov chain — making the connection between the learned noise predictor and the actual image generation loop explicit
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from training to inference, to explain how the trained model is used to generate new samples; contrast with the training algorithm to highlight the differences
suggested_caption: Sampling algorithm: iteratively denoise from pure noise x_T to generate image x_0. (Ho et al., 2020)

description: A generated Fashion MNIST image (likely a T-shirt) produced by the trained diffusion model, showing the final output of the reverse diffusion process after training on the 28x28 Fashion MNIST dataset
teaching_value: Provides concrete evidence that the implemented diffusion model works end-to-end, motivating students by showing a real generation result and grounding the entire tutorial in a tangible outcome
when_to_show: Show at the end of the tutorial as the culminating result, after explaining training and sampling; use it to discuss limitations (low resolution) and potential improvements
suggested_caption: T-shirt generated by the trained diffusion model on 28×28 Fashion MNIST dataset.

description: A taxonomy diagram showing the landscape of generative models, branching into categories including GANs, VAEs, Flow-based models, and Diffusion models, with their key characteristics and relationships illustrated.
teaching_value: Helps students situate diffusion models within the broader generative modeling ecosystem, understanding how they differ from GANs (adversarial training), VAEs (surrogate loss), and flow models (architectural constraints).
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a diffusion models lesson to establish prior knowledge and motivate why a new class of models is needed.
suggested_caption: Taxonomy of generative model families, including diffusion models. (Weng, 2021)

description: A diagram of the Markov chain for the forward and reverse diffusion process: left-to-right shows progressive noise addition from x0 to xT (forward), right-to-left shows iterative denoising from xT back to x0 (reverse), with annotated transition distributions q and p_theta.
teaching_value: Visually anchors the two core processes of DDPM — forward noising and learned reverse denoising — making the abstract Markov chain formulation concrete and showing how generation works by reversing noise.
when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the DDPM framework, before presenting the mathematical formulation of q(x_t|x_{t-1}) and p_theta(x_{t-1}|x_t).
suggested_caption: Forward (noising) and reverse (denoising) Markov chain in DDPM. (Ho et al., 2020)

description: A visualization of a diffusion model applied to 2D swiss roll data, showing the gradual transformation of the data distribution through forward diffusion steps and the learned reverse process reconstructing the original swiss roll shape.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, low-dimensional example of how diffusion models learn to reverse a noising process, making the abstract probability distribution transformation tangible before moving to image data.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the reverse diffusion process to give students a concrete geometric intuition before tackling high-dimensional image examples.
suggested_caption: Diffusion model learning to reverse noise on 2D swiss roll data. (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015)

description: Side-by-side pseudocode algorithms for DDPM training (Algorithm 1) and DDPM sampling (Algorithm 2), detailing the exact steps including noise prediction, loss computation, and iterative denoising with the learned model.
teaching_value: Bridges theory and implementation by showing the exact algorithmic steps, allowing students to map mathematical equations to concrete computational procedures for both training and inference.
when_to_show: Show after deriving the simplified ELBO/loss objective, to demonstrate how the math translates into a practical training loop and sampling procedure.
suggested_caption: DDPM training and sampling algorithms in pseudocode. (Ho et al., 2020)

description: A plot comparing linear beta scheduling (Ho et al., 2020) versus cosine-based beta scheduling (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021) over training timesteps, showing how the cosine schedule maintains more signal at the ends of the diffusion trajectory.
teaching_value: Illustrates why the choice of noise schedule matters: the cosine schedule avoids abrupt changes near t=0 and t=T, leading to better sample quality and lower NLL, teaching students about hyperparameter design in diffusion models.
when_to_show: Show when discussing improvements to DDPM, specifically when explaining why a cosine schedule outperforms a linear one for model log-likelihood.
suggested_caption: Linear vs. cosine beta schedules during diffusion training. (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021)

description: A bar chart comparing negative log-likelihood (NLL) in bits/dim across multiple likelihood-based generative models including improved DDPM, original DDPM, and other baselines on standard image benchmarks.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for the improvements gained by learned variance and cosine scheduling, teaching students to evaluate generative models quantitatively using NLL as a metric.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the improved DDPM techniques (learned variance, cosine schedule) to validate their effectiveness with quantitative results.
suggested_caption: NLL comparison of improved DDPM vs. other generative models. (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021)

description: Pseudocode algorithms for classifier-guided diffusion sampling with both DDPM and DDIM, showing how gradient signals from a separately trained classifier are incorporated into the reverse diffusion update step to condition generation.
teaching_value: Shows students exactly how external classifier gradients modify the score function during sampling, making the mathematical concept of classifier guidance concrete and implementable.
when_to_show: Show when introducing conditional generation with diffusion models, after explaining unconditional DDPM/DDIM, to contrast guided vs. unguided sampling.
suggested_caption: Classifier-guided sampling algorithms for DDPM and DDIM. (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021)

description: A line graph showing FID scores on CIFAR10 and CelebA datasets as a function of the number of sampling steps, comparing DDIM (eta=0, cyan) and DDPM (sigma-hat, orange), demonstrating DDIM's superior sample quality at fewer steps.
teaching_value: Quantitatively demonstrates DDIM's key advantage over DDPM: achieving lower FID (better quality) with far fewer sampling steps, teaching students the practical trade-off between speed and quality in diffusion inference.
when_to_show: Show when comparing DDIM to DDPM to motivate why deterministic implicit sampling is preferred for fast generation, especially when compute is limited.
suggested_caption: DDIM achieves lower FID than DDPM with fewer sampling steps. (Song et al., 2020)

description: A diagram illustrating the progressive distillation process, showing how a teacher model using N steps is distilled into a student model using N/2 steps iteratively, halving the required sampling steps each round.
teaching_value: Visually explains the iterative knowledge distillation strategy for accelerating diffusion sampling, helping students understand how a complex multi-step process can be compressed without retraining from scratch.
when_to_show: Show when introducing distillation-based methods for faster diffusion sampling, after students understand DDIM, to explain how step count can be halved iteratively.
suggested_caption: Progressive distillation halves sampling steps each iteration. (Salimans & Ho, 2022)

description: Side-by-side pseudocode comparison of Algorithm 1 (standard diffusion model training) and Algorithm 2 (progressive distillation training), with green highlights marking the specific changes introduced in the distillation procedure.
teaching_value: Enables students to precisely identify what changes between standard training and distillation training by using color-coded diffs, reinforcing understanding of the distillation objective and how the teacher guides the student.
when_to_show: Show alongside the progressive distillation diagram to give students the algorithmic details, especially useful for students who want to implement or reproduce the method.
suggested_caption: Standard training vs. progressive distillation algorithms, changes highlighted in green. (Salimans & Ho, 2022)

description: Diagram showing a diffusion trajectory from clean data x_epsilon to noisy data x_T, with arrows illustrating how the consistency function f maps any point on the trajectory back to the origin x_epsilon, demonstrating the self-consistency property.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the core property of consistency models — that any noisy intermediate state can be mapped directly to the clean data origin in a single step, making the abstract mathematical definition f(x_t, t) = f(x_t', t') = x_epsilon concrete and intuitive.
when_to_show: Show when introducing consistency models, immediately after defining the consistency function f: (x_t, t) → x_epsilon, to help students visualize why the self-consistency constraint enables fast sampling.
suggested_caption: Consistency models map any trajectory point directly to its clean-data origin. (Song et al., 2023)

description: A table or chart comparing consistency model performance across different configurations, varying distance metrics (LPIPS vs others), ODE solvers (Heun vs others), and number of discretization steps N, showing FID or similar quality scores for both consistency distillation (CD) and consistency training (CT).
teaching_value: Helps students understand which design choices matter most in consistency models by showing empirical ablation results, reinforcing that LPIPS distance, Heun ODE solver, and N=18 are the best-performing configuration for CD.
when_to_show: Show after explaining both CD and CT training objectives, when discussing practical implementation choices and hyperparameter sensitivity in consistency models.
suggested_caption: Ablation results for consistency models: LPIPS + Heun solver + N=18 is optimal. (Song et al., 2023)

description: A rate-distortion curve plotting compression rate (bits per dimension) on the x-axis against distortion on the y-axis, with annotated regions showing where perceptual compression and semantic compression occur, illustrating that most image bits encode perceptual details rather than semantic content.
teaching_value: Provides the theoretical motivation for latent diffusion models by showing that aggressive compression retains semantic content while discarding perceptual redundancy, justifying why running diffusion in latent space is both efficient and effective.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the LDM section, before describing the autoencoder architecture, to motivate why operating in latent space is a principled design choice rather than just a computational shortcut.
suggested_caption: Rate-distortion tradeoff showing perceptual vs. semantic compression stages. (Rombach & Blattmann et al., 2022)

description: Architecture diagram of the Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) showing: an encoder E compressing image x to latent z, a U-Net denoising model operating on z with cross-attention for conditioning inputs (text, semantic maps, etc.), and a decoder D reconstructing the image from the denoised latent.
teaching_value: Gives students a complete system-level view of LDM, showing how the autoencoder, diffusion process, and conditioning mechanism interact, making it easier to understand how pixel-space and latent-space diffusion differ architecturally.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the full LDM pipeline, after motivating latent-space diffusion with the rate-distortion plot, to give students a concrete architectural reference.
suggested_caption: LDM architecture: encoder compresses to latent, U-Net denoises, decoder reconstructs. (Rombach & Blattmann et al., 2022)

description: A pipeline diagram showing multiple diffusion models arranged in sequence at increasing image resolutions (e.g., 64x64 → 256x256 → 1024x1024), where each model upsamples and refines the output of the previous model, illustrating the cascaded diffusion approach.
teaching_value: Illustrates how cascaded diffusion models achieve high-resolution image generation by decomposing the problem into stages, helping students understand why this is more tractable than directly generating high-resolution images in a single model.
when_to_show: Show when introducing cascaded diffusion models as a strategy for scaling up generation resolution, to contrast with single-model approaches and motivate the need for conditioning augmentation between stages.
suggested_caption: Cascaded diffusion pipeline generates high-resolution images through sequential upsampling stages. (Ho et al., 2021)

description: Architecture diagram of unCLIP (DALL-E 2) showing the two-stage pipeline: a prior network that maps CLIP text embeddings to CLIP image embeddings, followed by a diffusion decoder that generates images conditioned on the image embedding, with CLIP encoders shown for both text and image inputs.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how CLIP embeddings are used as an intermediate representation to bridge text and image modalities, helping students understand the role of the prior and decoder in text-to-image generation and why this two-stage design enables high-quality, semantically aligned outputs.
when_to_show: Show when explaining unCLIP/DALL-E 2, after introducing CLIP, to help students trace how a text prompt flows through the system to produce a final image.
suggested_caption: unCLIP architecture: CLIP prior maps text to image embeddings for guided diffusion. (Ramesh et al., 2022)

description: The original U-Net architecture diagram showing a symmetric encoder-decoder structure with downsampling (left) and upsampling (right) paths, skip connections (gray arrows) between corresponding encoder and decoder feature maps, with channel counts labeled on top and spatial dimensions on the left.
teaching_value: Provides the foundational architectural reference for U-Net, which is the backbone of most diffusion models. Skip connections are critical for preserving spatial detail during upsampling, and understanding this architecture is prerequisite to understanding how diffusion models denoise images.
when_to_show: Show at the start of the model architecture section, before discussing modifications like cross-attention or ControlNet, as the baseline architecture that students need to understand first.
suggested_caption: U-Net encoder-decoder with skip connections; backbone of most diffusion model denoisers. (Ronneberger, 2015)

description: Architecture diagram of ControlNet showing the original locked U-Net on one side and a trainable copy of the encoder connected via zero-convolution layers, illustrating how external conditioning signals (e.g., edge maps, depth maps) are injected into the U-Net encoder layers through the sandwiched zero-convolution mechanism.
teaching_value: Visually explains the key ControlNet design innovation — using zero-initialized convolutions to safely add a trainable branch to a frozen pretrained model — helping students understand how conditional control is added without degrading the original model's capabilities.
when_to_show: Show when explaining ControlNet after introducing U-Net, to illustrate how the architecture is modified to accept spatial conditioning inputs while preserving pretrained weights.
suggested_caption: ControlNet adds trainable zero-convolution branches to frozen U-Net encoder layers. (Zhang et al., 2023)

description: Architecture diagram of the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) showing how noisy latent patches are tokenized and processed through transformer blocks with adaptive layer norm (adaLN) for timestep and class conditioning, followed by a linear decoder to predict noise or output patches.
teaching_value: Shows students how the transformer architecture replaces U-Net for diffusion modeling, highlighting the patch tokenization, conditioning via adaLN, and the scalability advantage of transformers — key for understanding why DiT achieves better compute efficiency at larger scales.
when_to_show: Show after explaining U-Net-based diffusion models, when contrasting transformer-based alternatives, to help students understand the architectural differences and the scalability motivation for DiT.
suggested_caption: DiT processes latent patches through transformer blocks with adaLN conditioning. (Peebles & Xie, 2023)
